summary:
Hungary faces rising SEN/BTMN identification [F-live-hungarian_education_system-01][F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01] amid contested causes: diagnostic-practice change, school-readiness rule tightening [F-live-hungarian_education_system-08][F-live-school_network_planning-04], grading-exemption reform [F-live-legal_and_governance-03], financing incentives [F-live-education_finance-08][F-live-legal_and_governance-07], and possible true prevalence increase [F-live-demography-03][F-live-international_comparison-03]. Six transformation paths define the option space, separable into empirical questions (what is driving the numbers, what would work) and value choices (how much disruption risk to accept, whose autonomy to constrain, how urgently to prioritize legal compliance versus administrative stability).

T1 (passive baseline) serves as an analytical counterfactual only; multiple lenses stress that inaction is not neutral because demographic contraction [F-live-demography-01][F-live-demography-02], workforce aging [F-live-demography-06], and unresolved ECtHR/EU obligations [F-live-legal_and_governance-09][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09] continue to compound. The constraint is that it forecloses no options but also fixes none.

T2 (measurement reconciliation) is low-risk and evidence-strong: it would decompose divergent series [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-01][F-live-portuguese_reform-01] and definitional artifacts [F-live-hungarian_education_system-03][F-live-education_finance-04], following precedent from Finland's tier-specific growth pattern [F-live-finnish_reform-02] and OECD diagnostic-practice findings [F-live-demography-05][F-live-international_comparison-03]. Its mechanism is diagnostic, not corrective — it leaves the sole gatekeeper [F-live-hungarian_education_system-04] and funding architecture [F-live-legal_and_governance-07] untouched, meaning better data does not by itself redistribute access.

T3 (tiered, non-categorical restructuring) targets the gatekeeping bottleneck directly [F-live-hungarian_education_system-04], modeled on Wales' ALN decline [F-live-international_comparison-06] and Finland's three-tier system [F-live-finnish_reform-01][F-live-international_comparison-07]. But Finland's persistent 4%-13% municipal variation after reform [F-live-finnish_reform-04] and its infrastructure dependence on dual-teacher co-teaching that Hungary lacks [F-live-international_comparison-10] limit transferability, while Poland's rapid, non-consensual 2016-2017 restructuring produced severe disruption, including a Warsaw placement crisis and teacher strikes [F-live-polish_reform-01][F-live-polish_reform-06][F-live-polish_reform-07][F-live-polish_reform-09].

T4 (finance redesign toward census-based allocation) rests on an untested incentive theory: no causal evidence links Hungary's per-pupil multiplier [F-live-education_finance-08] to the identification rise, and Finland's census-based shift correlated with reduced special placement [F-live-finnish_reform-03], while Poland instead expanded disability-weighted funding independent of prevalence [F-live-polish_reform-03][F-live-polish_reform-04], undercutting universality of the mechanism.

T5 (workforce expansion) addresses a well-documented, spatially concentrated shortage [F-live-hungarian_education_system-05][F-live-legal_and_governance-08][F-live-school_network_planning-05][F-live-school_network_planning-06], and is comparatively uncontroversial politically [F-live-political_feasibility-05], but rising training enrollment does not guarantee practicing supply [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-05][F-live-education_finance-07], and salary reforms may not target specialists specifically [F-live-portuguese_reform-03].

T6 (anti-segregation/adjacent-policy coordination) most directly engages unresolved legal exposure [F-live-legal_and_governance-09][F-live-legal_and_governance-10][F-live-political_feasibility-06][F-live-political_feasibility-09] and untouched channels like free school choice and selective tracks [F-live-hungarian_education_system-06][F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-08], drawing on Kertesi-Kézdi segregation findings [F-live-implementation_planning-08] and Roma overrepresentation evidence [F-live-equity_and_social_mobility-07][F-live-international_comparison-09]. Its constraint is a decade-plus record of institutional resistance and low enactment likelihood absent external enforcement [F-live-political_feasibility-08][F-live-political_feasibility-10], with Poland's parallel rollback of mainstreaming during structural reform as a cautionary analogue [F-live-polish_reform-02].

Decision-makers must choose between sequencing (measure first, then restructure, versus act now under uncertainty), between accepting disruption risk for structural gains versus incremental capacity-building, and between prioritizing legal-compliance urgency versus administrative continuity. The research most likely to shift the choice concerns the causal decomposition of identification drivers (rule changes, financing, diagnostic practice, true prevalence), whether Hungary's specific funding multiplier drives over-identification, whether a Finland-style tier would relabel existing students or capture genuine unmet need, and whether Roma misclassification is statistically distinguishable from general over-identification trends.
